item.title: my opinion please don't lynch me
item.content:encoded:
Goods: It's somewhat well written.
Follows the format and gets the idea across.
I like the background information, specifying the possible connections (her parents) on why she does this thing.

Bads: Cliché; it's literally "u touch u die lol."
The part where the D-class with "higher literature score" or whatever seems like an excuse to write some over-emotionalized poetry about the result of touching it; I would prefer the D-class describing it more "scientifically" or formally instead of speaking in free-form poetry.

Overall, it follows all of the rules of an SCP thing, and I feel like I shouldn't downvote just because I don't like the idea. So, neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reflective index isn't a thing, refractive index is.

From a quick google, a refractive index of 0 essentially lets light move infinitely fast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, I liked this a lot, especially the D-class' transcript. But it ending with "And then he turned into a box." felt a bit silly. Him just dying or even vanishing is creepy enough, but I can't help but imagine a cartoon sound effect as a box just plops into being where he laid.
There's also the "not composed of any atoms" bit which sounds very unscientific. I know it points to another SCP where, presumably, this phenomenon is explained in more detail, but my first reaction is to ask "Well, what then? What does it look like under a scanning tunneling microscope? And how is it so very light if it's entirely indivisible?" The text seems to not even acknowledge just how weird this object is.
Also, the D-class' post-mortem fate is only mentioned there, implying that he's the only one this happened to, but the previous description implies that there have been a lot of tests with SCP-2017. What then happened to all the others who touched it? The description mentions "other anomalous phenomena", but then gives no examples other than someone turning into a box. Other SCP documents usually provide some examples or even a full list of things that happened.
"Other anomalous phenomena" just sounds like weasel words. I'd be fine if that happened to everyone. I'd be fine if D-3500 was the only one who had any post-mortem anomalies. But this is just too vague for me.

No vote from me. I love the premise and the D-class' log is extremely evocative, but there's just a few things that feel a bit unfitting for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This right here is how you do a humanoid. Great job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff Notice: this user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little dissapointed they didnt try to find someone with symptoms like her parents and see if they werent affected. there have to be d-class like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey! I have derealization/depersonalization as well and I really liked this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A perfectly rectangular prism… that'd be a cube, wouldn't it?

And something with a reflective (refractive) index of zero… that'd be totally transparent, wouldn't it? Like air?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something I would have liked to see is testing with D-class that already had dissociative disorders. There's an ambiguous implication that her parents were "safe" because they were already on that spectrum, so I'd like to see if that holds true in a more general sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had just assumed that to be based on generalized paranoia. Not necessarily a psychotic symptom either. I mean, if your daughter can actually give people a dissociative disorder just by touching them, would it really seem so strange that there are other impossibilities out there? And well, there's already that movie Men In Black, why can't there really be groups like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject displayed literary talent above the general D-class population.

I'm glad they're screening for that now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +!'d this to 100. Congrats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is awesome. One of my new favorite SCPs. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the girl's basically a doorway between this world and another? Excellent idea and very touching the way you brought through the human aspect of SCP-2017. The crib-transformation thing didn't really make sense or add to the story. Also, wouldn't the prism itself become an SCP in it's own right or at least an extension of this one? Apart from that I loved it. +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for me. The interview was the clincher, really—-I can't be the only one who got the feels when SCP-2017 asked if she was there because she'd been bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's a girl that makes you k-hole when she touches you?

Jokes aside, I really liked this. I felt the descriptions of disassociation to be frighteningly vivid, genuinely scary, and very accurate (reading your other comments, I can see why), and I thought the interview was a masterpiece. Very well written.

There were some bits I didn't like, though. I didn't like the final stage of the effects, the 'other place', with the mysterious and maybe-malevolent entities. I also didn't like the test subject turning into a prism, I thought that was a bit contrived. I think it would be much stronger with just the disassociation. It seems a little bit like you thought that wouldn't be enough, and added a bit more danger for danger's sake, which is never a good idea.

+1 as it is, but I think it could be a much stronger, tighter, and more unsettling piece relatively easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview really clenched it for me. I love the idea of having to explain the reason for containment to an SCP, and you handled it beautifully.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2017-Triggered Derealization Disorder

SCP-2017-Triggered Dissociation Disorder

You have these back to back. It seems like one needs to be changed to match the other.

I really like this concept, and the article was a pleasure to read. Except the part about the cot/body changing into a prism. That felt kind of weird and tacked-on to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but one sentence is sorta bothering me:

SCP-2017's mother and father were aware of SCP-2017's properties and attempted to conceal them from notice from a group such as the Foundation.

This sounds like it's implying that knowledge of organizations dealing with the paranormal are a widely known phenomenon in-universe. That, or the couple came to this knowledge because their daughter attracted attention from these groups prior to the Foundation's involvement. This their any explaining/re-wording that could clarify this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
BEAUTIFUL. MissMercurial, your take on psychiatric/psychological topics is excellent. Love it love it love it. Marking it for early translation to the SCP-ES. : D

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's actually a question I was asking myself. In universe, no. Out-universe, I actually have a dissociative disorder, and a lot of the inspiration for both articles came from my symptoms. The logs from the affected D-class in this article, for example, express a lot of what I experienced during my most recent dissociative episode. Art plagiarizes life…

As for the nitpick…well, trifles make perfection, and perfection is no trifle, huh? I'll fix it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hrm. I rather like this. Is this connected at all to your other SCP article? The 'place' seems to share a number of common themes with it though… not quite.

Also, super-nitpick. The name of last name of the kid is 9 black boxes, but the last name of the parents is 8 black boxes, I'm assuming that they're supposed to have the same last name.

item.title: Mistakes?
item.content:encoded:
"when a human makes unprotected skin contact with with SCP-2017"
Are there supposed to be 2 'with's there?
"is a African-American human female.'
Isn't it an*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't Doctor Baxter that nervous tapping woman from your tale?

Upvoted, by the way. Lovely.

E: Yes. Yes she was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I meant to imply that they believe she died in the hospital. I see now I didn't make that clear anywhere. I'll change it soon.

Hmm. Didnt think of blood/hair/ other testing. I might add that in as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good job, but I have a couple of questions.

What happened to the parents? Why not make them into the handlers of the child? If not, then maybe you could mention that they were given amnestics and are being observed (maybe mom is pregnant again…).

The child shows symptoms of anemia-what does his blood work show? Does his blood, hair, nails, sputum, etc show similar effects on people? A little more testing would be nice.

All in all, a good article. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well shit. Didn't see that coming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love a good humanoid. The interview feels believable, and I feel sorry for her because she's the unfortunate vector of a bizarre force beyond her control, but the article itself keeps the cold, clinical tone while telling a very human-feeling story, and *inarticulate squeeing noises*.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Top shelf, top shelf. Really though, my favorite new SCP, especially with the allusions to the "other place". I love it when things are actually creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview and the child's back-story is what makes this one. Upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty cool! This is something that really isn't a "super power" but more of an anomaly that seems otherworldly, which I like!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it particularly the interview
